does: This paper develops a data-centric post-training pipeline that uses interpretability techniques to analyze preference datasets before optimization begins. The authors apply interpretability protocols to identify and characterize latent concepts that distinguish preferred from dispreferred model generations, making these concepts explicit for fine-grained feedback. They frame several existing interpretability-based training methods as approaches to shaping rewards through feature or data interventions. The key contribution is demonstrating that this interpretability-first approach can diagnose problematic signals in preference datasets that would otherwise remain hidden, including spurious correlations that lead to undesirable behaviors like over-stylization and sycophancy that emerge during standard reward optimization.
connects: This work builds on the growing recognition that post-training through scalar reward optimization is an imperfect abstraction that obscures what models actually learn from preference data. It extends recent research in mechanistic interpretability and model internals analysis to the post-training setting, drawing on techniques for probing and understanding learned representations. The paper responds to prior work highlighting failures of reward-based optimization such as reward hacking and behavioral distortions, and connects to research on preference learning and RLHF. It also builds on emerging work using interpretability to improve training, combining insights from interpretability-based interventions with classical data-centric machine learning approaches.
enables: This work opens several future directions for post-training alignment and safety. It establishes interpretability as a foundational tool for auditing preference datasets before deployment, enabling practitioners to verify that training signals encode desired behaviors rather than spurious correlations. The framework could be extended to develop automated concept-identification systems that flag problematic patterns at scale. More broadly, it suggests a new paradigm where interpretability protocols guide the design of training objectives directly, potentially replacing scalar reward maximization with concept-aware training methods. This could lead to more transparent post-training pipelines where stakeholders understand exactly which behaviors models are incentivized to learn.
